title: The world is made for you
subtitle: And what to say to children about difference
paragraphs: When a child is struggling, we want to help them feel better, and one of the ways that we do that is often to tell them that it’s not them, it’s the world. Particularly for autistic children, there’s a culture of telling them that the problem is that the world just isn’t made for them, that it was designed by neurotypical people for their needs and theirs alone.
Here’s why I don’t think that’s a good idea.

Children believe what we tell them, and autistic children in particular often take this literally. When a child hears ‘the world isn’t made for you’, this can mean a whole host of things to them, but one of them is ‘You can’t have it’.
Think about when children hear this phrase in other contexts.
‘Those biscuits weren’t made for you’ – means, you can’t eat them (maybe because I baked them for your granny).
‘That TV programme wasn’t made for you’ - means, you can’t watch it (maybe because it’s inappropriate).
‘Those clothes weren’t made for you’ – means, you can’t put them on (maybe because they are too small).
They all say, you can’t participate. Stop. Those don’t belong to you.
I don’t think this is the right message to be sending any child about the world they live in.

Yes, it’s true that the world can be a (very) challenging place for autistic children and their families. Yes, it’s true that life is much harder for some people than others – but what we say to children matters, and we want them to feel that they are an important part of the world, and that together we’ll help them explore it.
Plus it’s not actually true that the world is designed for the benefit of most people. Whether they are autistic or not, many people have a very tough time making their way in the world.
How about ‘We’re all different, and that’s part of what makes the world so exciting’.
Or ‘There are lots of different places in the world, and we’ll find the places where you feel most comfortable’.
Or ‘Everyone finds some things harder and easier – you find school hard sometimes but look at how great you are at playing Mario Kart’.
Or ‘Things are really difficult sometimes, but it’s not always this hard’.

We need to build in flexibility and the possibility of change to what we say to our children, because they need to know that there’s hope. They need to know that just because things are hard now doesn’t mean they’ll be this hard forever. They need to hear that the world is for them just as much as to anyone else.
And they need to know that we believe that they belong. Let’s start with that.
